local_names.chinese_simplified: 萨摩萨炸角（马铃薯豌豆馅）
local_names.chinese_traditional: 薩摩薩炸角（馬鈴薯豌豆餡）

food_subgroup: Deep-fried wheat pastry with spiced potato and pea filling

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Samosa. Nearest parent is a fried filled pastry or deep-fried dumpling. A new FoodOn concept is recommended.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pastry products — deep-fried wheat pastry with vegetable filling (closest parent)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of 2 samosas weighs approximately 150g and consists of two triangular deep-fried pastry cones filled with a spiced mashed potato and pea mixture, served with tamarind chutney and mint-coriander chutney for dipping.

nutrient_source.title: Samosa (potato, peas) — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, Food Code F05020106
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), Hyderabad
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Exact match found in ICMR-NIN under food code F05020106 — Samosa (aloo), fried, standard Indian preparation.

gi_source: University of Sydney GI Database — fried wheat flour pastry with potato filling; GI estimated from refined wheat and potato components.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium approximately 477mg per 2-piece serving (150g) — about 21% of the 2300mg daily recommended limit; relatively low-sodium for a fried snack.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension may consume samosas occasionally; the tamarind and mint chutneys served alongside are additional sodium sources that should be used sparingly.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined wheat flour pastry has a high GI (~65); potato filling adds high GI starch; per-serving glycaemic load of approximately 29 is significant for a snack-sized portion.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Diabetic patients should limit samosa consumption to an occasional treat, preferring baked over deep-fried versions, and pairing with a protein-rich accompaniment to moderate the glycaemic response.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying creates pro-inflammatory compounds (acrylamide, advanced glycation end products); refined wheat is pro-inflammatory at high consumption; however, cumin and coriander provide anti-inflammatory phytocompounds.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should limit fried samosa consumption; baked samosas are a preferable alternative, and the anti-inflammatory spices in the filling provide modest benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Samosa is a classic Indian fried snack of a triangular wheat flour pastry shell filled with a spiced mashed potato and pea mixture seasoned with cumin, coriander, garam masala, and green chilli, then deep-fried until golden and crisp. It is typically served with sweet tamarind chutney and fresh mint-coriander chutney. Samosas are vegetarian and vegan in the standard aloo (potato) preparation, and are widely available at Indian restaurants, mamak stalls, and bakeries across Singapore as a popular snack at all times of day.
common_user_queries: how many calories in a samosa

confidence_reason: Exact match found in ICMR-NIN under food code F05020106 — Samosa (aloo), fried, standard Indian preparation.
notes: The standard potato-pea samosa is vegetarian and vegan (no dairy in the dough in the standard recipe). Cholesterol is 0.0g as this is a vegan dish. Vitamin B12 is 0.0 (no animal products). Meat samosas (chicken or lamb) will have significantly different nutritional profiles. Chutney accompaniments are not included in the per_100g values.
